APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses character-level neural machine translation and morphology learning, a core NLP/ML topic appropriate for top venues. It evaluates on large, standard WMT En-Fr and En-Cs corpora and newstest sets, compares to strong baselines (bpe, char, char2char, C2W), and reports BLEU, which matches community expectations. The problem formulation, methods (hierarchical character/word encoders and decoders), and evaluation standards are aligned with what top NLP/ML conferences expect, and the work is positioned within the relevant NMT research community. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural machine translation at the character level—and proposes a hierarchical architecture to learn morphology, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. It evaluates on large, standard WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora with established baselines (bpe, char, C2W, char2char) and BLEU metrics, matching community expectations for data and evaluation. The problem formulation, experimental setup, and positioning (analysis of morphology, training efficiency, comparison to state-of-the-art) make it well targeted to the venue’s audience despite minor stated limitations (e.g., explicit segmentation and limited character set).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses character-level neural machine translation and morphology learning, a core NLP/ML topic appropriate for top venues. It evaluates on large, standard WMT En-Fr and En-Cs corpora and newstest sets, compares to strong baselines (bpe, char, char2char, C2W), and reports BLEU, which matches community expectations. The problem formulation, methods (hierarchical character/word encoders and decoders), and evaluation standards are aligned with what top NLP/ML conferences expect, and the work is positioned within the relevant NMT research community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural machine translation at the character level—and proposes a hierarchical architecture to learn morphology, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. It evaluates on large, standard WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora with established baselines (bpe, char, C2W, char2char) and BLEU metrics, matching community expectations for data and evaluation. The problem formulation, experimental setup, and positioning (analysis of morphology, training efficiency, comparison to state-of-the-art) make it well targeted to the venue’s audience despite minor stated limitations (e.g., explicit segmentation and limited character set).

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a distinct architecture (DCNMT) that combines a novel two-RNN word encoder (character-wise representations plus a BiRNN producing weights) with a hierarchical two-level character decoder (HGRU), which is more than a straightforward application of existing character-level NMT models. It explicitly targets morphology learning and claims efficiency and early BLEU gains versus a wide set of baselines (bpe, C2W, char2char, etc.), showing clear differentiation from prior work. These design choices represent a non-trivial extension of existing methods and would likely be informative to experts, though they build on a well-studied area rather than introducing a wholly new problem formulation. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new architecture (DCNMT) combining a two-RNN word encoder and a hierarchical two-level character decoder (six RNNs across four layers) which is a non-trivial architectural departure from prior character/ subword NMT models like C2W, char2char and BPE baselines. The authors explicitly differentiate their contributions via comparisons to multiple baselines and analyses showing learned morpheme boundaries and morphology, and they train the model end-to-end on large WMT corpora. Because character-level NMT is a well-studied problem, the work is notably novel and clearly differentiated but not a radical paradigm shift, and some stated limitations (need for explicit segmentation, restricted character set) temper its originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a distinct architecture (DCNMT) that combines a novel two-RNN word encoder (character-wise representations plus a BiRNN producing weights) with a hierarchical two-level character decoder (HGRU), which is more than a straightforward application of existing character-level NMT models. It explicitly targets morphology learning and claims efficiency and early BLEU gains versus a wide set of baselines (bpe, C2W, char2char, etc.), showing clear differentiation from prior work. These design choices represent a non-trivial extension of existing methods and would likely be informative to experts, though they build on a well-studied area rather than introducing a wholly new problem formulation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new architecture (DCNMT) combining a two-RNN word encoder and a hierarchical two-level character decoder (six RNNs across four layers) which is a non-trivial architectural departure from prior character/ subword NMT models like C2W, char2char and BPE baselines. The authors explicitly differentiate their contributions via comparisons to multiple baselines and analyses showing learned morpheme boundaries and morphology, and they train the model end-to-end on large WMT corpora. Because character-level NMT is a well-studied problem, the work is notably novel and clearly differentiated but not a radical paradigm shift, and some stated limitations (need for explicit segmentation, restricted character set) temper its originality.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental evidence (BLEU comparisons on WMT En-Fr and En-Cs, architecture and hyperparameters reported) and model analyses asserting learned morphology. However, important methodological details raise concerns: restricting to the 120 most frequent characters (mapping others to <unk>) and the stated need for explicit segmentation undermine the claim of a fully character-level model and introduce potential biases. Additionally, claims about training efficiency lack reported statistical tests or clear runtime comparisons, so some experimental support is incomplete. | B(4): The paper's main claims are supported by experiments on large WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora and comparisons against reasonable baselines (bpe2bpe, char, char2char, C2W), with BLEU reported and hyperparameters provided, which gives credible evidence for the proposed DCNMT architecture and its training efficiency. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or detailed controls ensuring fair training-time/evaluation budgets across baselines, and the design choices that could bias results (e.g. mapping all but the 120 most frequent characters to <unk> and the stated need for explicit segmentation) are potential methodological weaknesses. These are minor issues that do not fatally undermine the experimental findings, but they warrant clarification.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental evidence (BLEU comparisons on WMT En-Fr and En-Cs, architecture and hyperparameters reported) and model analyses asserting learned morphology. However, important methodological details raise concerns: restricting to the 120 most frequent characters (mapping others to <unk>) and the stated need for explicit segmentation undermine the claim of a fully character-level model and introduce potential biases. Additionally, claims about training efficiency lack reported statistical tests or clear runtime comparisons, so some experimental support is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are supported by experiments on large WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora and comparisons against reasonable baselines (bpe2bpe, char, char2char, C2W), with BLEU reported and hyperparameters provided, which gives credible evidence for the proposed DCNMT architecture and its training efficiency. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or detailed controls ensuring fair training-time/evaluation budgets across baselines, and the design choices that could bias results (e.g. mapping all but the 120 most frequent characters to <unk> and the stated need for explicit segmentation) are potential methodological weaknesses. These are minor issues that do not fatally undermine the experimental findings, but they warrant clarification.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent and relevant baselines (bpe2bpe, C2W, CNMT, char, bpe2char, char2char) using the same WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora and BLEU evaluation. It reports both early-training (BLEU after one epoch) and final results (stating comparability to state-of-the-art), analyzes morphological behavior, and notes limitations (segmentation, character set, no monolingual data). Related work coverage is described as adequate, and the comparisons appear fair and well-analyzed. | B(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent baselines (bpe2bpe, C2W, CNMT, char, bpe2char, char2char) on large WMT En-Fr and En-Cs corpora using BLEU. Comparisons are made under the same data/dev/test conditions and metrics, and the authors report both early training advantages and final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art, acknowledging when prior methods perform comparably. They also analyze differences (e.g., learned morpheme boundaries and morphology) and report adequate related-work coverage, indicating the comparisons are comprehensive and fair.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent and relevant baselines (bpe2bpe, C2W, CNMT, char, bpe2char, char2char) using the same WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora and BLEU evaluation. It reports both early-training (BLEU after one epoch) and final results (stating comparability to state-of-the-art), analyzes morphological behavior, and notes limitations (segmentation, character set, no monolingual data). Related work coverage is described as adequate, and the comparisons appear fair and well-analyzed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of recent baselines (bpe2bpe, C2W, CNMT, char, bpe2char, char2char) on large WMT En-Fr and En-Cs corpora using BLEU. Comparisons are made under the same data/dev/test conditions and metrics, and the authors report both early training advantages and final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art, acknowledging when prior methods perform comparably. They also analyze differences (e.g., learned morpheme boundaries and morphology) and report adequate related-work coverage, indicating the comparisons are comprehensive and fair.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—operating at character/subword level to avoid large vocabularies while learning morphology—by proposing a deep character-level architecture (DCNMT). Empirical claims that DCNMT trains more efficiently and achieves higher BLEU than a bpe baseline after one epoch on En–Fr and En–Cs, with final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art, indicate results that could change how researchers approach character-level NMT and morphology modeling. The demonstrated ability to learn morpheme boundaries opens clear follow-on research directions and potential reuse, though practical limitations (need for explicit segmentation, restricted character set) may temper immediate widespread adoption. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of character-level neural MT and morphological modeling by proposing a novel DCNMT architecture (two-RNN word encoder + hierarchical character decoder) that avoids large-vocabulary issues. Empirically it reports faster training and higher BLEU than bpe after one epoch and final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art on large WMT En–Fr and En–Cs corpora, and shows the model can learn morpheme boundaries—evidence it could change how researchers approach morphology and subword modeling. However, practical limitations (need for explicit segmentation to obtain meaningful word representations, a restricted 120-character vocabulary mapping others to <unk>, and reliance only on parallel data) constrain generality, so the work is likely to have strong but not transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—operating at character/subword level to avoid large vocabularies while learning morphology—by proposing a deep character-level architecture (DCNMT). Empirical claims that DCNMT trains more efficiently and achieves higher BLEU than a bpe baseline after one epoch on En–Fr and En–Cs, with final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art, indicate results that could change how researchers approach character-level NMT and morphology modeling. The demonstrated ability to learn morpheme boundaries opens clear follow-on research directions and potential reuse, though practical limitations (need for explicit segmentation, restricted character set) may temper immediate widespread adoption.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of character-level neural MT and morphological modeling by proposing a novel DCNMT architecture (two-RNN word encoder + hierarchical character decoder) that avoids large-vocabulary issues. Empirically it reports faster training and higher BLEU than bpe after one epoch and final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art on large WMT En–Fr and En–Cs corpora, and shows the model can learn morpheme boundaries—evidence it could change how researchers approach morphology and subword modeling. However, practical limitations (need for explicit segmentation to obtain meaningful word representations, a restricted 120-character vocabulary mapping others to <unk>, and reliance only on parallel data) constrain generality, so the work is likely to have strong but not transformative impact.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates DCNMT on large WMT En-Cs and En-Fr corpora, compares against multiple baselines (bpe, C2W, CNMT, char variants including bpe2char and char2char), reports training efficiency and BLEU improvements (notably after one epoch) and final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art, and includes analyses showing learned morpheme boundaries. Related work is described as adequate, and hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility. However, the summary does not report systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components, and code availability is not mentioned; while some limitations are stated (segmentation requirement, limited character set, no monolingual data), deeper failure-case analysis appears limited. Overall this is solid experimental work with minor gaps in ablation and reproducibility details. | B(4): The paper evaluates DCNMT on large WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora, compares against multiple strong baselines (bpe, char, char2char, C2W, etc.), reports BLEU results, presents morphological analyses, and provides hyperparameters and stated limitations—supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity studies to isolate the contributions of the hierarchical/dual-RNN components, and the asserted training-efficiency advantage is not shown with detailed quantitative evidence in the provided summary. Reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters, but code is not mentioned.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates DCNMT on large WMT En-Cs and En-Fr corpora, compares against multiple baselines (bpe, C2W, CNMT, char variants including bpe2char and char2char), reports training efficiency and BLEU improvements (notably after one epoch) and final BLEU comparable to state-of-the-art, and includes analyses showing learned morpheme boundaries. Related work is described as adequate, and hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility. However, the summary does not report systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components, and code availability is not mentioned; while some limitations are stated (segmentation requirement, limited character set, no monolingual data), deeper failure-case analysis appears limited. Overall this is solid experimental work with minor gaps in ablation and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates DCNMT on large WMT En–Cs and En–Fr corpora, compares against multiple strong baselines (bpe, char, char2char, C2W, etc.), reports BLEU results, presents morphological analyses, and provides hyperparameters and stated limitations—supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity studies to isolate the contributions of the hierarchical/dual-RNN components, and the asserted training-efficiency advantage is not shown with detailed quantitative evidence in the provided summary. Reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters, but code is not mentioned.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with the expected flow (introduction, detailed method, experiments, conclusion) and states the main contributions early (novel DCNMT architecture that learns morphology). Notation and methodological description are sufficiently precise to describe the six recurrent networks and hierarchical decoder, and important experimental details (datasets, hyperparameters, baselines) are reported. Writing quality is adequate overall, though the architecture description is dense and could be streamlined for readability (e.g., the two-RNN word encoder and hierarchical decoders are complex and may benefit from clearer figures or examples). | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from introduction to method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (the DCNMT architecture, training efficiency, and BLEU improvements) are stated early. The methodology is described in detail (two-RNN word encoder, bidirectional sentence encoder, hierarchical character decoder across six RNNs), and experiments report datasets, baselines and BLEU scores, which supports reproducibility (hyperparameters are reported). Writing is adequate and mostly unambiguous, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., explicit segmentation and limited character-vocabulary constraints are noted as limitations) that prevent it from being exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with the expected flow (introduction, detailed method, experiments, conclusion) and states the main contributions early (novel DCNMT architecture that learns morphology). Notation and methodological description are sufficiently precise to describe the six recurrent networks and hierarchical decoder, and important experimental details (datasets, hyperparameters, baselines) are reported. Writing quality is adequate overall, though the architecture description is dense and could be streamlined for readability (e.g., the two-RNN word encoder and hierarchical decoders are complex and may benefit from clearer figures or examples).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression from introduction to method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (the DCNMT architecture, training efficiency, and BLEU improvements) are stated early. The methodology is described in detail (two-RNN word encoder, bidirectional sentence encoder, hierarchical character decoder across six RNNs), and experiments report datasets, baselines and BLEU scores, which supports reproducibility (hyperparameters are reported). Writing is adequate and mostly unambiguous, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., explicit segmentation and limited character-vocabulary constraints are noted as limitations) that prevent it from being exceptional.

main_claims: Proposes a novel deep character-level neural machine translation architecture (DCNMT) that learns morphology via a two-RNN word encoder and a hierarchical character-level decoder.
DCNMT consists of six recurrent networks across four layers and avoids the large-vocabulary issue of word-level models by operating at character (subword) level.
The model is more efficient in training than word-based and conventional character-based models and obtains higher BLEU than a bpe-based model after one epoch on En-Fr and En-Cs.
Final BLEU score of DCNMT is reported to be comparable to state-of-the-art systems, and analyses show the model can learn morpheme boundaries and morphology.
_summary.methodology: They build a hierarchical model with a source word encoder composed of two RNNs (one producing character-wise representations rt and a BiRNN producing weights wt), a bidirectional sentence encoder, a first-level decoder that computes a state st combining context and a target-word feedback rY, and a second-level character decoder (HGRU) that generates target characters; the model is trained end-to-end on parallel corpora.
datasets: WMT En-Cs parallel corpus (15.8M sentence pairs)
WMT En-Fr parallel corpus (12.1M sentence pairs)
limitations: Requires explicit segmentation to obtain meaningful word representations (the need for explicit segmentation is stated).
Only the 120 most frequent characters per language are used; characters outside this list are mapped to a special <unk> token.
They do not use any monolingual corpus (only parallel corpora were used).
